title: Newly discovered PamStealer isn't your typical macOS malware
gist: Jamf researchers uncovered PamStealer, a stealthy macOS infostealer that masquerades as the Maccy clipboard app, uses a JXA dropper and Rust second stage, and validates stolen passwords locally via macOS's PAM interface to evade detection while also targeting Ethereum accounts.

insights.claim: PamStealer, discovered by Jamf, is a never-before-seen macOS infostealer that validates a victim's login password locally through macOS's Pluggable Authentication Modules (PAM) interface before exfiltrating it, avoiding the process spawns (dscl, security, osascript) that commodity stealers use and defenders detect.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Local PAM validation removes a detection signal, raising the bar for endpoint-security vendors serving Mac fleets.
insights.quote: there is no call out to dscl, security, osascript or any spawned process to verify the password, as many commodity macOS stealers do
insights.locator: PamStealer shows a native password prompt

insights.claim: The malware uses a self-contained JavaScript for Automation (JXA) downloader that stages payloads via native Objective-C APIs rather than shell commands like curl or zsh, producing a quieter execution chain than typical commodity stealers.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Native-API staging over shell commands is a detection-evasion pattern security tooling must adapt to catch.
insights.quote: the AppleScript executes a self-contained JavaScript for Automation (JXA) downloader that retrieves and stages the payload using native Objective-C APIs

insights.claim: PamStealer delays sensitive actions like the Full Disk Access request for up to forty minutes so its activity does not coincide with launch, masquerades as Finder, and encrypts its command-and-control traffic to stay hidden.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Time-delayed prompts break the launch-correlation heuristic defenders rely on, demanding longer behavioral monitoring windows.
insights.quote: holding back prompts like the Full Disk Access request for as long as forty minutes so its activity does not line up with launch
insights.locator: PamStealer combines a recently emerging delivery surface

insights.claim: PamStealer is delivered via a disk image impersonating Maccy, a real Mac clipboard manager, and prompts the user to press Command-R after double-clicking, which executes malicious AppleScript directly and bypasses macOS's com.apple.quarantine protections.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: The Command-R social-engineering step defeats Gatekeeper-style quarantine warnings, a user-behavior attack pattern to train against.
insights.locator: When a user, expecting to install a trustworthy

insights.claim: After capturing a valid password, PamStealer shows a decoy message claiming the file is damaged and can't be installed to prevent the victim from suspecting compromise.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: The fake-failure decoy extends dwell time before victims react, a psychological technique worth flagging in user awareness training.
insights.locator: Once the target enters the correct password

insights.claim: PamStealer's second stage is a lean Mach-O binary written in Rust for Apple-CPU Macs, an uncommon choice versus the Swift, Go, and Objective-C typical of macOS infostealers, and it reads SQLite databases directly and targets Ethereum accounts.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Rust adoption and crypto-wallet targeting signal maturing, financially-motivated macOS malware tooling.
insights.locator: The second stage is a lean Mach-O file

insights.claim: Jamf frames PamStealer as evidence that commodity macOS stealers are evolving toward quieter execution chains and native implementations that reduce detection while staying compatible with standard macOS features.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: A trend thesis that macOS threats are professionalizing, relevant to demand for Mac-focused endpoint detection like Jamf's.
insights.quote: commodity macOS stealers continue to evolve, adopting quieter execution chains and native implementations that reduce traditional detection opportunities
